name: Split-flight imperial stair
description: Imperial staircase in which a single broad lower flight rises along the central axis to a half-height landing where it splits into two narrower flights climbing in opposite directions to the upper storey. The wide first flight, four to six blocks across, invites a crowd to ascend together; the half-landing acts as a stage facing the room below; the upper flights, each two or three blocks wide, hug the side walls and arrive at symmetric balconies that frame whatever lies on the far wall. This Y-shaped figure was codified in Renaissance and Baroque palaces and reappears in opera-house lobbies, civic buildings, and grand chateaus, where its strong ceremonial axis turns the stair itself into the room's main event.
